title: Routes of Exchange
description: This exercise will cover an overview of trade routes across the Indian Ocean and the Sahara Desert with a focus on the themes of ECN, CDI, ENV, TEC, and GOV. Each of these themes will have a type and each type will scale 1 or 2 items, for a total of 12.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the relationship between trade in the Indian Ocean and technology?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Technological innovations expanded trade, while the exchange of ideas increased technological innovation.
New agricultural techniques drove the need for technological innovation due to shortages in food along the Indian coastline.
Technological innovations allowed for an increase in trade, which fueled knowledge of new technologies through conquest.
New agricultural techniques were introduced but hindered technological innovation due to the spread of invasive diseases.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following historical examples best supports the assertion that trade networks in the Indian Ocean fostered the growth of coastal city-states?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Diasporic communities, like the Gujarati community, used their common language and beliefs to conduct long-distance business and consolidate power to create Gujarat along the Indian coast.
Diasporic communities, like the Jewish community, used Hebrew norms and customs to spread Judaism along the coast of India, consolidating Jewish influence in the region’s political sphere.
Interactions with indigenous populations allowed Chinese maritime explorers to consolidate power for the Ming Dynasty and conquer several city-states along popular trade routes.
Interactions with indigenous communities along the Swahili Coast led to organized rebellion, creating communities that consolidated power and defended local customs against cultural hegemony.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is evidence of cultural exchange brought about by trade networks in the Indian Ocean from 1200 to 1450 CE?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Along the coast of East Africa, Bantu languages like Swahili adopted several Arabic words due to cultural interactions with Islamic merchants.
Along the coast of India, agricultural techniques acquired through trade with China allowed for rice to become a major Indian export.
Along the coast of China, Islamic merchants grew in such numbers that Arabic script is sometimes seen in Chinese texts from the Ming Dynasty.
Along the coast of China, Hinduism became widely practiced and was eventually integrated into the culture of coastal China.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

> When we arrived at the foreign countries, barbarian kings who resisted transformation and were not respectful we captured alive, and bandit soldiers who looted and plundered recklessly we exterminated. Because of this, the sea routes became pure and peaceful and the foreign peoples could rely upon them and pursue their occupations in safety. All of this was due to the aid of the goddess.

Source: Zheng He, Inscribed on a granite tablet,  Liujiagang (now Liuhe), China. 

**Which of the following best describes the broad historical context for the development described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The relatively harmonious nature of trade in the Indian Ocean allowed for robust transfers of culture and technology.
The growth of trade in the Indian Ocean led to the death of large portions of indigenous peoples due to the spread of disease.
The relatively harmonious nature of trade in the Indian Ocean led to an industrial boom in China, fueled by resources acquired along trade routes.
The growth of trade in the Indian Ocean was largely due to the Ming Dynasty’s conquests along coastal trade routes and their exercise of military force to regulate them.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

> When we arrived at the foreign countries, barbarian kings who resisted transformation and were not respectful we captured alive, and bandit soldiers who looted and plundered recklessly we exterminated. Because of this, the sea routes became pure and peaceful and the foreign peoples could rely upon them and pursue their occupations in safety. All of this was due to the aid of the goddess.

Source: Zheng He, Inscribed on a granite tablet,  Liujiagang (now Liuhe), China.

**In which of the following ways does the excerpt above illustrate an effect of the Indian Ocean trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Diplomatic interactions between merchants encouraged significant cultural and technological transfers between distant regions.
Diplomatic interactions between merchants increased industrial output for many coastal city-states along the Indian Ocean.
The Ming Dynasty acquired new sailing technology, leading to cultural and technological hegemony in the Indian Ocean.
The Ming Dynasty used relationships established along trade routes to conquer many city-states and expand its cultural influence.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following environmental factors most directly influenced the development of the Indian Ocean trade network?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: monsoon winds
tide schedules
seasonal rains
temperate weather

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did environmental factors influence cultural exchanges in port cities along the Indian Ocean trade network? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Arab merchants wrote travel guides detailing how to plan voyages around monsoon winds.
Arab merchants accompanied sailors to impart knowledge of local waterways at various ports.
Chinese sailors developed oral histories describing common dangers that were adopted along the Swahili Coast.
Chinese sailors wrote manuals on how to navigate the Indian Ocean, leading to the adoption of Chinese characters into Arabic script.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did the Berbers play in facilitating trade in the Saharan Desert?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They developed the camel saddle, enabling camels to carry more goods for longer distances.
They established an empire that encompassed most of the Saharan Desert and allowed for goods to be traded at port cities.
They were known pastoralists who engaged in trade in order to fund their transition to a more sedentary lifestyle.
They created the astrolabe, which allowed for more precise direction and locating capabilities.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the image below to answer the question that follows.**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following developments most directly contributed to Mansa Musa’s pilgrimage across the Sahara?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Black and white print that shows a crowd of people and animals moving through a desert landscape.
options.caption: Mansa Musa on His Way to Mecca. Mansa Musa, Emperor of Mali, went on a pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324. He is said to have traveled with an entourage of 60,000. Public Domain.

choices.content: Camel caravans made travel across the Sahara viable.
Trade networks motivated Mansa Musa to go find more gold.
Communication networks brought news of European conquests of the Holy Land.
Horses arrived in Africa and could carry people and goods long distances.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains the relationship between trans-Saharan trade and the growth of the Empire of Mali?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Empire of Mali exercised strict control over the movement of merchants and their trade routes, enriching their empire.
The Empire of Mali constructed trade routes across the Sahara Desert in order to gain access to more raw materials.
The Empire of Mali utilized new technologies to successfully navigate trade throughout the Sahara Desert, while other empires failed to do so.
The Empire of Mali exempted themselves from trans-Saharan trade, instead staying isolated and maintaining their wealth independently.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

> On Fridays, if a man does not go early to the mosque, he cannot find a corner to pray in, on account of the crowd. It is a custom of theirs to send each man his boy [to the mosque] with his prayer-mat; the boy spreads it out for his master in a place befitting him [and remains on it] until he comes to the mosque. Their prayer-mats are made of the leaves of a tree resembling a date-palm, but without fruit.

>Another of their good qualities is their habit of wearing clean white garments on Fridays. Even if a man has nothing but an old worn shirt, he washes it and cleans it, and wears it to the Friday service. Yet another is their zeal for learning the Koran by heart. They put their children in chains if they show any backwardness in memorizing it, and they are not set free until they have it by heart. I visited the qadi in his house on the day of the festival. His children were chained up, so I said to him, "Will you not let them loose?" He replied, "I shall not do so until they learn the Koran by heart.

Source: Ibn Battuta, Travels in Asia and Africa 1325-1354

**Based on Ibn Battuta’s assessment of the Empire of Mali, which of the following best describes the broad historical context around trans-Saharan trade routes? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Ibn Battuta, Travels in Asia and Africa 1325-1354, trans. and ed. H. A. R. Gibb (London: Broadway House, 1929) PDF of 1929 edition [Internet Archive]. Public Domain.

choices.content: They allowed for the spread of Islam, which provided a common language and shared value systems between people from diverse areas.
They created a network of wealthy communities, which enabled leisure travel among wealthier merchants.
They enabled the cultural hegemony of the Empire of Mali, which forced all merchants to convert to Islam upon their arrival.
They developed the gold trade, which led to the enslavement of many indigenous peoples by foreign merchants.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows. **

> On Fridays, if a man does not go early to the mosque, he cannot find a corner to pray in, on account of the crowd. It is a custom of theirs to send each man his boy [to the mosque] with his prayer-mat; the boy spreads it out for his master in a place befitting him [and remains on it] until he comes to the mosque. Their prayer-mats are made of the leaves of a tree resembling a date-palm, but without fruit.

> Another of their good qualities is their habit of wearing clean white garments on Fridays. Even if a man has nothing but an old worn shirt, he washes it and cleans it, and wears it to the Friday service. Yet another is their zeal for learning the Koran by heart. They put their children in chains if they show any backwardness in memorizing it, and they are not set free until they have it by heart. I visited the qadi in his house on the day of the festival. His children were chained up, so I said to him, "Will you not let them loose?" He replied, "I shall not do so until they learn the Koran by heart.

Source: Ibn Battuta, Travels in Asia and Africa 1325-1354

**Based on the excerpt above, which of the following best explains how the expansion of the trans-Saharan Trade influenced communication?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Ibn Battuta, Travels in Asia and Africa 1325-1354, trans. and ed. H. A. R. Gibb (London: Broadway House, 1929) PDF of 1929 edition [Internet Archive]. Public Domain.

choices.content: Cities within empires became centers of learning and scholarship, and trade routes enabled scholars to move around the world and exchange knowledge.
Cities within empires created new technologies that allowed for the exchange of information almost instantaneously.
Trans-Saharan trade routes developed a colloquial language that allowed for merchants from diverse areas to communicate more effectively.
Trans-Saharan trade routes became areas of mutual wealth, which incentivized innovations in communication technology, such as the printing press.
